coredata.dc:title: Study on the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Zheng, Hu-Zhi
Long, Yi-Juan
Du, Juan
Hao, Jian-Yu
Wang, Ling-Ling
Zhou, Dong-Bo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we prepared water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots with diameter about 1.5nm from cheap commercial lampblack. These fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are stable toward photobleaching and stable in water for more than half a year without fluorescence decrease. In order to improve its fluorescence properties, we passivated these nanoparticles with bisamino-terminated polyethylene glycol (PEG1500N). Therefore, both fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime increased after this progress. In addition, the passivated carbon dots were more inert to solvent than the bare one and showed different responses to pH change.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Passivation
Fluorescence quantum yield
Fluorescence lifetime

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The emission spectra of bare CDs (a) and passivated CDs (b) excited at different wavelengths.
The absorption (ABS) and fluorescence spectra of bare CDs (a) and passivated CDs (b).
The plot of fluorescence intensity of bare CDs (▴) and modified CDs (■) vs. concentration.
pH effects on fluorescence of bare CDs (A) and passivitated CDs (B). Britton–Robinson buffer solution was used to adjust the pH value.
Solvent effects on fluorescence of bare CDs (A) and passivated CDs (B).
Metal ion effects on fluorescence of bare CDs (A, pH 6.0) and modified CDs (B, pH 7.0). The concentrations of metal ion are 1×10−4 M and 1×10−6 M.
Fluorescence lifetime of CDs excited at 280nm.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The percentage of τ 1, τ 2, and τ 3 is about 40%, 35% and 25%, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence lifetime of CDs excited at 460nm.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The percentage of τ 1, τ 2, and τ 3 is about 40%, 25% and 35%, respectively.

head.ce:title: Study on the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Beibei, Chongqing 400715, PR China. Tel.: +86 23 68253312; fax: +86 23 68254000.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we prepared water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots with diameter about 1.5nm from cheap commercial lampblack. These fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are stable toward photobleaching and stable in water for more than half a year without fluorescence decrease. In order to improve its fluorescence properties, we passivated these nanoparticles with bisamino-terminated polyethylene glycol (PEG1500N). Therefore, both fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime increased after this progress. In addition, the passivated carbon dots were more inert to solvent than the bare one and showed different responses to pH change.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic fluorchromes have been widely used as fluorescent tags for many years, however, these traditional small-molecule fluorescent tags suffered from the disadvantages of poor photostability, relatively low fluorescence intensity [1]. Fluorescent nanoparticles are able to overcome these shortcomings, which make them share a wide range of applications in the biomedical field, such as to be used in vivo or biological labeling, as well as FRET [2–7]. The most widely used fluorescent nanoparticles—quantum dots (QDs) [8–14]— are a kind of semiconductor nanoparticles which developed in recent years. QDs have unique fluorescent properties, such as strong fluorescence, high photostability, long fluorescence lifetime, broad excitation spectra, narrow and tunable emission spectra [1,15–19], all these distinct them from the commonly used fluophores and inorganic/organic dyes. For both in vitro and in vivo uses, however, the known toxicity [18], potential environmental hazard and relative large size of them [19] may represent serious limitation. Therefore, the search for more benign nanomaterials of similar optical properties is a worthwhile yet challenging undertaking.

Carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes [20–24], fullerenes [25,26] and nanodiamonds [27] have become popular fields for chemists, physicists, and materials scientists. More recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)—a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials with interesting photoluminescence properties—have been prepared by several chemists [28–34]. Xu et al. [28] derived fluorescent carbon dots from single-walled carbon nanotubes. Sun et al. [29] prepared carbon dots via laser ablation of graphite, surface oxidation with nitric acid and subsequent covalent grafting of organic moieties. These light-emitting dots have relatively better fluorescence properties, and can be used for multi-photon bioimaging [30]. Zhou et al. [31] obtained fluorescent carbon dots from multi-walled carbon nanotubes with electrochemical methods. Liu et al. [32] prepared fluorescent carbon dots from candle soots. And Bourlinos et al. [33,34] produced carbogenic dots through thermal oxidation of suitable molecular precursors. All these carbon dots showed high photostability against photobleaching, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, relative small size and low molecular weight.

In this paper, we use commercial lampblack, which is a primary material of Chinese ink, to prepare fluorescent CDs (i.e. bare CDs) and passivated the bare CDs with O,O′-bis (3-aminopropyl) polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG1500N) (i.e. passivated CDs). What interests us is that the fluorescent properties of bare CDs can be improved by passivating with PEG1500N. These two kinds of CDs are both highly water-soluble and stable toward photobleaching, and they are stable in water for more than half a year. Furthermore, the carbon nanoparticles with size about 1.5nm reported here fluorescence in the visible, when stimulated with a wide range of excitation wavelengths.

F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France), UV-2450 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (JEM 2010, JEOL, Japan), Z383K centrifuge (Hermle, Germany), dialysis bags (Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China), ultra-filtration tube (Millipore Corporation, Billerica, MA 01821, USA, cutoff=30kDa). Lampblack (Anhui Jixi Hukaiwen ink industry Co., China) is a primary material of Chinese ink, which is collected from burning tung oil. O,O′-bis (3-aminopropyl) polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG1500N) (Fluka), fluorescein (Shanghai SSS Reagent Co., Ltd.). Ultra-pure water was prepared with a Milli-Q-Plus system (18.2MΩ) and used throughout. All other reagents were used as received.

After being washed with acetone and dried at 110°C, 0.5g of lampblack was added into 150ml of HNO3 (5mol/l), the mixture was refluxed at 140°C for 12h. And then, the black solution was neutralized with sodium carbonate, and loaded into dialysis bags (cutoff molecular weight 8000–14,000) for dialysis against ultra-pure water for two days to purify the carbon dots. Acetone was added in the purified solution and the bare CDs were collected by centrifugation at 16,000rpm for 15min. The precipitation was dried under nitrogen flow and dispersed in ultra-pure water, then ultra filtered to remove big particles. At last, the obtained brown solution, i.e. bare CDs solution, was kept at 4°C until use.

0.2g of O,O′-bis (3-aminopropyl) polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG1500N) was mixed with 50ml of CDs solution (about 0.5mg/ml), and the mixture was heated to 120°C for 72h under nitrogen. After the reaction, the PEG-passivated CDs were cooled to room temperature followed by treating with dialysis and ultrafiltration.

The absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectrum of CDs were measured at room temperature. Absorption spectra were recorded by a UV-2450-PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra were collected by an F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The size and dispersivity of CDs were determined by a JEOL-JEM 2010FEF TEM (Japan), for which samples were dropped onto a copper grid. The size distribution was determined by counting more than 100 particles.

The quantum yield of CDs was calculated by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities and absorbency values (<0.1) of the carbon samples with the references quinine sulfate (literature Φ =0.54) and fluorescein (literature Φ =0.95) [35].

The fluorescence lifetime was measured by a Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France). 280nm and 460nm were chosen as excitation wavelengths and emissions at 520nm were collected.

The average diameter of bare CDs was about 1.5nm (see Fig. 1 ) (more than 100 particles were calculated). After passivated with PEG1500N, however, the TEM micrographs became blurry. Therefore, TEM micrographs of PEG1500N passivated CDs had not shown here. But we can conjecture that the passivated CDs are a little bigger than bare CDs.

The fluorescence intensity changes linearly with concentration at low concentration (as shown in Fig. 4 ). At higher concentration, however, the fluorescence intensity rolls over. The high concentration effect is certainly associated with self-quenching by the CDs nanoparticles themselves.

The quantum yield of bare and modified CDs was calculated with quinine sulfate (excited at 310nm) and fluorescein (at 445nm) as the reference compounds. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence quantum yield of PEG1500N passivated CDs is higher than the bare CDs. This result is consistent with Sun's report [29].

The fluorescence lifetime of bare and modified CDs was measured by TCSPC method, and the excitation and emission wavelength were 280/460nm and 520nm, respectively. The lifetime of these two kinds of CDs excited at different wavelengths listed in Tables 2 and 3
. It shows that the lifetime of passivated CDs is longer than the bare one.

Improving fluorescence characteristic of fluorescent nanoparticles is very important. For the photoluminescent carbon dots reported here, PEG1500N passivation can enhance both quantum yield and quantum lifetime of CDs. For which a widely accepted mechanism is the present of surface energy traps that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of the surface passivation [29]. In addition, PEG can prevent CDs from affecting by quench reagents and environmental factors.

As shown in Fig. 5 , both the bare CDs and passivated CDs are sensitive to pH. The photoluminescence intensity reaches to the maximal value at pH 6.0 for bare CDs and pH 7.0 for passivated CDs, respectively. The surface of bare CDs changes dramatically after passivation. Different surface groups cause different responses to pH value.

The fluorescence intensity of bare CDs and passivated CDs is also solvent-dependent (see Fig. 6 ). The fluorescence intensity of bare CDs in water is about seven times higher than in ethanol or acetonitrile and decreases to almost zero in acetone (Fig. 6A). Fluorescence intensity of passivated CDs in ethanol, acetonitrile and acetone is about half of the intensity in water (Fig. 6B). It seems that bare CDs are more sensitive to solvent effects than passivated CDs. It might be interpreted that the polymer shield onto passivated CDs can prevent CDs from fluorescence-quenching of external factors.

Metal ion effects on bare CDs and passivated CDs were also investigated. 1×10−4 M of Mn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Cu2+ quenched bare CDs fluorescence with 20–40% intensity decrease; 1×10−4 M of other metal ions did not quench fluorescence (see Fig. 7A). These metal ions showed similar effects on passivated CDs (see Fig. 7B).

In summary, we synthesized and purified water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots from cheap commercial lampblack. The nanoparticles are stable for more than half a year under ambient condition and can be excited by a wide rang of wavelengths (from 260nm to 500nm), thereby emitting at different wavelengths (excited from 420nm to 500nm). The CDs contain carboxylic acid groups on their surface, which allows functionalization with biomacromolecules through N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) chemistry. The method reported herein provides a convenient way to prepare CDs by using a common carbon resource, and these CDs can be passivated with PEG1500N to improve fluorescence properties. Both fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime increased as a result of the surface passivation. In addition, these two kinds of CDs (bare CDs and passivated CDs) are sensitive to pH, and show highest fluorescence properties in near neutral solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Scientific Program – Nanoscience and Nanotechnology of PR China (No. 2006CB933100) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 20707017). The authors wish to thank Prof. Huang Chengzhi and Ms Guo Fenglin (Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, China) for help in fluorescence lifetime measurement.
